revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:富士信仰]]
{{ill|Fujisan Sengen Faith|2=ja|3=富士浅間信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisan Sengen Faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士信仰}} (富士浅間信仰)
[[File:Asamano Ookami.jpg|thumb|250px|right|'''{{ill|Asama Daibosatsu|ja|浅間大菩薩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asama Daibosatsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s resting place stone monument located on the upper part of the Hitoana, regarded as the residence of the deity ({{ill|Hitoana Fuji-kō Ruins|ja|人穴富士講遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitoana Fuji-kō Ruins|1=Hitoana Fuji-ko Iseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11376150}})''']]'''Fuji Faith''' (富士信仰) is a Shinto belief ({{ill|Shinto Faith|ja|神祇信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 古神道|1=Ko-Shintō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3096508}}) in the deity of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}. It is one of the forms of {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=Mountain Worship|1=Mountain worship}}.
== Overview == [[File:Fujinomiya Hongu Sengen Taisha Honden.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|en|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|qid=Q653180|lt=Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha|1=Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha}}]]Fuji Faith involves the veneration and worship of Mount Fuji in some form – for example, regarding the mountain itself as a deity. A representative form is '''Asama Faith''' (浅間信仰, also known as '''Fuji Asama Faith'''). Other notable forms include {{ill|Murayama Shugendo|ja|村山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shugendo|1=Murayama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11523090}} and {{ill|Fujiko (religion)|en|Fujiko (religion)|qid=Q11456201|lt=Fuji Sect|1=Fujiko (religion)}}. {{main|{{ill|Murayama Shugendō|ja|村山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shugendō|1=Murayama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11523090}}|Fujiko (religion)}}[[File:Omuro＿Sengen＿Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Fujiyama Shimomiya Komuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiyama Shimomiya Komuro Asama Shrine|1=Omuro Sengen Shrine (Yamanashi)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11459659}}. A form of worship of Mount Fuji is observed.]]
When broadly dividing the history of Fuji Faith, one finds: an initial era of primitive mountain worship – when shrines began to be established to enshrine the mountain deity (called “Fukujishin” in {{ill|Fudoki of Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fudoki of Hitachi Province|1=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481687}}, “Reimoya Shin-kōmon” in the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Manyōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}, and “Asama Daimyō” in {{ill|Miyako no Yoshika|en|Miyako no Yoshika|qid=Q11643817|lt=Tsuruga|1=Miyako no Yoshika}}’s {{ill|Fujisan Chronicle|ja|富士山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisan Chronicle|1=Fujisan Chronicle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131331453}}); an era in which {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} entered Japan and developed as a form of mountain forest Buddhism integrating earlier kami-worship concepts, marked by the appearance of ascetics (called Fuji Dōsha) whose mountain training took on a sacred mountain character; an era during the late {{ill|平安|en|平安|qid=Q18668577|lt=Heian period|1=平安}} when mountain ascetics became more organized and established sacred training sites; an era from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanbokuchō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} and the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} in which common people increasingly climbed Mount Fuji as Fuji pilgrims or as part of the Fuji Sect; and finally, the era from the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} and {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} periods onward, characterized by the age of {{ill|Sect Shinto|en|Sect Shinto|qid=Q1532150|lt=Sect Shinto|1=Sect Shinto}}.<ref name="sato">『{{ill|Folk Religion|ja|民俗宗教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Folk Religion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民間信仰|1=Folk religion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1068640}}』 Volume 2, p.117 “Various Aspects of Mountain Worship in Local Communities” by {{ill|Eiichi Sato|en|Eiichi Sato|qid=Q5349195|lt=Sato Eiichi|1=Eiichi Sato}}, Tōkyōdō Publishing, 1989</ref>
== Asama Faith == This section details Asama Faith in particular. Early on, the idea of worshipping Mount Fuji itself emerged, and shrines dedicated to the mountain deity, Asama Daimyō, were established in various regions at the foothills of the mountain.<ref name=sato/> Asama Faith originates from this tradition.
The core of Asama Faith is the {{ill|Sengen shrine|en|Sengen shrine|qid=Q3471785|lt=Asama Shrine|1=Sengen shrine}}, a shrine enshrining Asama Daimyō – regarded as the spirit of Mount Fuji. Approximately 1,300 such shrines (Fuji shrines) are distributed nationwide, mainly in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} and {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|qid=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. The primary shrine is considered to be {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|en|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|qid=Q653180|lt=Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha|1=Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha}} (located in {{ill|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|en|Fujinomiya, Shizuoka|qid=Q496072|lt=Fujinomiya City|1=Fujinomiya, Shizuoka}}, Shizuoka Prefecture), which covers most of the area above the 8th station of Mount Fuji (see details at Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha).<ref>{{ill|Tōguchi Honmitsu Fuji Asama Shrine|Tōguchi Honmitsu Fuji Asama Shrine|Tōguchi Honmitsu Fuji Asama Shrine|2=ja|3=東口本宮冨士浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōguchi Honmitsu Fuji Asama Shrine|1=Higashiguchi Hongu Fuji Sengen Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11526304}} in {{ill|Suntō District, Shizuoka|en|Suntō District, Shizuoka|qid=Q1142259|lt=Suntō District|1=Suntō District, Shizuoka}} and {{ill|Oyama, Shizuoka|en|Oyama, Shizuoka|qid=Q726418|lt=Oyama Town|1=Oyama, Shizuoka}}, or alternatively {{ill|Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine|lt=Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine|lt=Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Asama Shrine|Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine|lt=Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine|lt=Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Asama Shrine|Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine|lt=Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine|lt=Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Asama Shrine|2=ja|3=北口本宮富士浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Asama Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北口本宮冨士浅間神社}} in {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|qid=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida City|1=Fujiyoshida}}, are also considered primary by some views.</ref> Note that the original shrine of Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha, {{ill|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine|en|Yamamiya Sengen Shrine|qid=Q11467749|lt=Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha Yamamiya Asama Shrine|1=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine}}, is a rare form of shrine that does not possess a main building, exemplifying the ritual style of Fuji Faith.
It is generally held that Asama Daimyō is none other than {{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|en|Konohanasakuya-hime|qid=Q1781862|lt=Princess Konohananosakuya|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}. The reason why the enshrined deity of Asama Shrines came to be identified with Konohananosakuya-hime is attributed to her involvement in the myth of her birth (as recounted in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, specifically the section {{ill|Konohananosakuya-hime's Birth|Konohananosakuya-hime's Birth|Konohananosakuya-hime's Birth|2=ja|3=天孫降臨#古事記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohananosakuya-hime's Birth|1=Tenson kōrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1084733}}), which led to her association with fire – being identified as the deity of flame ({{ill|Fire worship|en|Fire worship|qid=Q2638573|lt=Fire God|1=Fire worship}}) after a miraculous birth in fire. However, according to the shrine legend of Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha, she is regarded as a water deity ({{ill|Suijin|en|Suijin|qid=Q3137449|lt=Water God|1=Suijin}}) who calms fire. The exact time when Mount Fuji’s deity came to be identified with Konohananosakuya-hime remains unclear. In many Asama Shrines, the primary deity is alternatively Ōyamatsumi ({{ill|Ōyamatsumi|en|Ōyamatsumi|qid=Q386563|lt=Great Mountain Deity|1=Ōyamatsumi}}), the father of Konohananosakuya-hime, or Iwanagahime ({{ill|Iwanaga-hime|en|Iwanaga-hime|qid=Q8191705|lt=Rock Long Princess|1=Iwanaga-hime}}), her sister. Some Asama Shrines also feature a distinctive complex shrine structure known as {{ill|Asama-zukuri|Asama-zukuri|Asama-zukuri|2=ja|3=浅間造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asama-zukuri|1=Sengen-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11557481}}. Through syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, Asama Daimyō is also sometimes called Asama Daibosatsu.
Mount Fuji has frequently erupted, causing damage to the people living at its foothills. Consequently, many Asama Shrines enshrine Konohananosakuya-hime as the sacred body ({{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|qid=Q327532|lt=Sacred Object|1=Shintai}}) to appease volcanic activity, considering her as either a fire deity or a deity of water virtue.
=== Etymology of "Asama" === There are various theories regarding the etymology of the name of Asama Shrines.<ref>{{ill|Miyaji Naoki|Miyaji Naoki|Miyaji Naoki|2=ja|3=宮地直一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyaji Naoki|1=Naokazu Miyaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11453535}}『History of Asama Shrines』, Meicho Publishing</ref> * One theory posits that “asama” is an archaic word referring to a volcano. * Another theory suggests that “Asama” designates a fierce, fiery deity – and during the Edo period Mount Fuji (a volcano) and {{ill|Mount Asama|en|Mount Asama|qid=Q242730|lt=Asama-yama|1=Mount Asama}} were regarded as the same deity. * A third theory claims that “Asama” signifies {{ill|Mount Aso|en|Mount Aso|qid=Q733710|lt=Aso Mountain|1=Mount Aso}}, the result of a Kyushu-origin legend fusing with primitive faith. * Yet another theory holds that “Asama” derives from the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|qid=Q101828|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu people}}, meaning either “a burning rock that emits fire” or “the depths of a swamp.” In Southeast Asian languages, similar words related to volcanoes or hot springs exist – for example, in Malay, “asa” means “smoke” and “ma” means “mother,” and these were applied to Mount Fuji. * Finally, one theory suggests that when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|en|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|qid=Q941979|lt=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} relocated Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha to its present site, he sought a new shrine name. At that time, many cherry trees grew naturally around the Yūtama Pond of Asama Taisha, and because of the strong association with cherry blossoms at the subsidiary shrine ({{ill|Setsumatsusha|en|Setsumatsusha|qid=Q1071160|lt=Sessha|1=Setsumatsusha}}) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} – namely, {{ill|Asakuma Shrine (Ise)|Asakuma Shrine (Ise)|Asakuma Shrine (Ise)|2=ja|3=朝熊神社 (伊勢市)|1=Asakuma Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakuma Shrine (Ise)|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakuma Shrine|qq=}} – the local people began referring to it as “Asama no Kaminoyashiro,” and thus the name was adopted for Asama Shrines.
== History == === Establishment of Fuji Faith === In the region surrounding Mount Fuji, several ritual sites from the late {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|qid=Q52824|lt=Jomon period|1=Jōmon period}} – such as the {{ill|Sengo ruins|en|Sengo ruins|qid=Q11405456|lt=Sengo ruins|1=Sengo ruins}} (in Fujinomiya City, Shizuoka Prefecture) and the {{ill|Ushiishi Ruins|Ushiishi Ruins|Ushiishi Ruins|2=ja|3=牛石遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ushiishi Ruins|1=Ushiishi Ruins|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11570211}} (in Tsuru City, Yamanashi Prefecture) – have been excavated. These sites feature arrangements of stones ({{ill|Stone circle|en|Stone circle|qid=Q1935728|lt=Stone Circles|1=Stone circle}}, known as “haishi”) and have been argued by scholars to be related to Fuji Faith.<ref name="fujisengen">{{ill|Dare2|Dare2|Dare2|2=ja|3=誰2|lt=unknown|lt_ja=unknown|lt_de=unknown|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dare2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, date=August 2014</ref> If correct, this suggests that the prototype of Fuji Faith existed at least prior to the Jomon period.
The popularization of mountaineering was a major factor in garnering popular devotion to Mount Fuji. One of the earliest records of climbing the mountain is attributed to {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|En no Gyōja|qid=Q198946|lt=Ascetic|1=En no Gyōja}}. The {{ill|Nihon Genpō Zen'aku Reii-ki|Nihon Genpō Zen'aku Reii-ki|Nihon Genpō Zen'aku Reii-ki|2=ja|3=日本現報善悪霊異記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Genpō Zen'aku Reii-ki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本国現報善悪霊異記|1=Nihon Ryōiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1606838}} states, “At night, proceeding to Suruga and ascending the peak,” indicating a connection between the ascetic and Mount Fuji. Similarly, in the {{ill|Fujisan Chronicle|Fujisan Chronicle|Fujisan Chronicle|2=ja|3=富士山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisan Chronicle|1=Fujisan Chronicle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131331453}} attributed to {{ill|Miyako no Yoshika|en|Miyako no Yoshika|qid=Q11643817|lt=Tsuruga|1=Miyako no Yoshika}}, it is recorded, “There once was an ascetic who managed to climb its summit,” showing numerous accounts linking the ascetic to Mount Fuji.
A figure known as the “Founder of Mount Fuji’s Opening” is {{ill|Matsudai|Matsudai|Matsudai|2=ja|3=末代|lt=Matsudai, the Senior Monk|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsudai|1=Matsudai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11519635}}. In the {{ill|Honchō Seiki|en|Honchō Seiki|qid=Q871866|lt=Honchō Seiki|1=Honchō Seiki}} (compiled by {{ill|Fujiwara no Michinori|en|Fujiwara no Michinori|qid=Q711600|lt=Shin-sei|1=Fujiwara no Michinori}}), it is stated: “There was a monk in Suruga Province called Fuji Shōnin, whose name was Matsudai; he ascended Mount Fuji several hundred times and constructed a Buddha pavilion at the summit, known as Dainichiji.” His act of building a temple at the summit is seen as a manifestation of his faith—a significant turning point in Fuji Faith. Matsudai is also regarded as the organizer of Shugendō, and his influence played a major role in the evolution of Fuji Faith.
In the {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu Reigenki|Jizō Bosatsu Reigenki|Jizō Bosatsu Reigenki|2=ja|3=地蔵菩薩霊験記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu Reigenki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (compiled by {{ill|Jitsuei|Jitsuei|Jitsuei|2=ja|3=実睿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jitsuei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), it is written: “They call him Matsudai. He worshipped the sacred peak of Fuji in Suruga Province…” (excerpt omitted) – this passage suggests a connection between Matsudai and {{ill|Murayama Shugendo|Murayama Shugendo|Murayama Shugendo|2=ja|3=村山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Shugendo|1=Murayama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11523090}}, leading to the view that Matsudai established Murayama Shugendo.<ref name="fujisengen">Hirano Eiji, {{ill|Fuji Asama Faith|Fuji Asama Faith|Fuji Asama Faith|2=ja|3=富士浅間信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Asama Faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士信仰|1=Fujisan Sengen Faith|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11455920}}, Yūzankaku Publishing</ref> Murayama Shugendo is one of the earliest organized expressions of Fuji Faith among the people.
Furthermore, in the late Heian period, the {{ill|Ryōjin Hishō|en|Ryōjin Hishō|qid=Q3010584|lt=Ryōjin Hishō|1=Ryōjin Hishō}} (compiled by {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|qid=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}}) states, “The sacred sites in all directions include, among others, the mountain of Fuji in Suruga and its training grounds…” indicating that by the end of the Heian period, Mount Fuji was already an object of devotion.<ref name="fujisengen"/></ref>
=== Bestowal of Divine Rank to Asama Daimyō === In the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052905|lt=Nihon Buntoku Tennō Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}} (compiled by {{ill|Fujiwara no Mototsune|en|Fujiwara no Mototsune|qid=Q1397196|lt=Fujiwara Mototsune|1=Fujiwara no Mototsune}} and others), it is recorded that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Ninju|en|Ninju|qid=Q1373077|lt=Jinju|1=Ninju}} (853 AD), Asama Kami was elevated to the Rank of a renowned deity.
Furthermore, in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}} (compiled by {{ill|Fujiwara no Tokihira|en|Fujiwara no Tokihira|qid=Q2994735|lt=Fujiwara Tokihira|1=Fujiwara no Tokihira}} and others), it states, “In Suruga Province, Asama Kami was Ranked as Shōsanmi,” indicating that in the 1st year of {{ill|Jōgan|en|Jōgan|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} (859 AD), he was promoted to the Rank of Shōsanmi.
Also, the same source records, “In Fuji District, the Great Mountain of Asama Daimyō, holding the Rank of Shōsanmi…” From these records, it is inferred that the state, in order to curb volcanic activity, had Asama Kami enshrined – which eventually became the present-day {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|en|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|qid=Q653180|lt=Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha|1=Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha}}.<ref>{{Cite book|language=ja|author=Kubota Atsushi|title=Fuji: The Source of Faith and Art|year=2009|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|en|Shogakukan|qid=Q785697|lt=Shogakukan|1=Shogakukan}}|pages=24}}</ref> Thus, it is believed that the first shrine related to Mount Fuji was established in Suruga Province.<ref name="fujisengen"/></ref>
In the report from the 6th year of Jōgan (864 AD) in August, it is recorded: “A low-Ranking official from Kai Province stated that the fire of Mount Fuji in Suruga was determined by divination using turtle shells, and that the enshrined priests and officials of Asama Myōjin were duly observing ritual purity.” This shows that in Suruga Province there were already priests and officiants enshrining “Asama Myōjin.”
In the report from the 7th year of Jōgan (865 AD) in December, it is recorded: “An Asama Myōjin shrine established in Yatsushiro District of Kai Province was listed as an official shrine, with appointed priests and officials, and rituals were conducted regularly.” This served as a turning point for the establishment of Asama Shrines on the Kai side as well. Thus, due to concerns over eruptions, Asama Faith gradually expanded.
Note that the names “Asama Kami”, “Asama Daimyō”, and “Asama Myōjin” vary according to era and record.
=== Relationship with Shugendō === In the {{ill|Gikeiki|en|Gikeiki|qid=Q2600904|lt=Yoshitsune-ki|1=Gikeiki}} (author unknown), it is written: “When the yamabushi met and discussed the peak of enlightenment, the state of Mount Shakyamuni Tathāgata, the significance of the Eight Great Kongo Children, as well as the peak of Fuji and the prayers of the yamabushi, someone would answer with dazzling clarity…” indicating that the relationship between Mount Fuji and Shugendō was widely recognized.<ref name="fujisengen"/></ref>
== Shrines Involved in Fuji Faith == * {{ill|Komagome Fuji Shrine|Komagome Fuji Shrine|Komagome Fuji Shrine|2=ja|3=駒込富士神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komagome Fuji Shrine|1=Komagome Fuji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11668442}} (located in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo Metropolis|1=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Bunkyō|en|Bunkyō|qid=Q212713|lt=Bunkyo Ward|1=Bunkyō}}, {{ill|Hon-Komagome|Hon-Komagome|Hon-Komagome|2=ja|3=本駒込|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hon-Komagome|1=Hon-Komagome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11521068}}) – One of the oldest Fuji Sect organizations, established by the {{ill|Town Fire Brigade|Town Fire Brigade|Town Fire Brigade|2=ja|3=町火消|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Town Fire Brigade|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 火消|1=Hikeshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11566931}}. The famous proverb “{{ill|One Fuji, Two Hawks, Three Eggplants|One Fuji, Two Hawks, Three Eggplants|One Fuji, Two Hawks, Three Eggplants|2=ja|3=一富士、二鷹、三茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/One Fuji, Two Hawks, Three Eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 初夢|1=Hatsuyume|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1588971}}” is said in modern times to originate from this shrine, although no documentary evidence exists to support this theory. According to this modern theory, the origin is attributed to the presence of a {{ill|Falconer's Mansion|Falconer's Mansion|Falconer's Mansion|2=ja|3=鷹匠屋敷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Falconer's Mansion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the vicinity and the local specialty of the {{ill|Komagome Eggplant|Komagome Eggplant|Komagome Eggplant|2=ja|3=駒込茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komagome Eggplant|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Others == Although not directly related to Fuji Faith, {{ill|Nichiren-shū|en|Nichiren-shū|qid=Q206611|lt=Nichiren Buddhism|1=Nichiren-shū}} and {{ill|Fuji Monryū|Fuji Monryū|Fuji Monryū|2=ja|3=富士門流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Monryū|1=Fuji lineage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11456263}} (also known as Nikkōmonryū) are also based on Mount Fuji. Following the dying wish of the sect founder {{ill|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478en|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478qid=Q311478|lt=Nichiren}}, who stated that “the main ritual platform of the sect should be established on Mount Fuji (summary),” {{ill|Taiseki-ji|en|Taiseki-ji|qid=Q654134|lt=Daishiji Temple|1=Taiseki-ji}} was built at the foot of Mount Fuji. Moreover, influential temples and sects established by Nichiren’s prominent disciple {{ill|Nikkō Shōnin|en|Nikkō Shōnin|qid=Q373169|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō Shōnin}} and his followers – namely, {{ill|Kamijō Daishiji Temple|Kamijō Daishiji Temple|Kamijō Daishiji Temple|2=ja|3=上条大石寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamijō Daishiji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大石寺|1=Taiseki-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q654134}}, {{ill|Kitayama Honmonji Temple|Kitayama Honmonji Temple|Kitayama Honmonji Temple|2=ja|3=北山本門寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitayama Honmonji Temple|1=Kitayama Honmon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11401463}}, {{ill|Nishiyama Honmonji Temple|Nishiyama Honmonji Temple|Nishiyama Honmonji Temple|2=ja|3=西山本門寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishiyama Honmonji Temple|1=Nishiyama Honmon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11627852}}, {{ill|Koizumi Kuonji Temple|Koizumi Kuonji Temple|Koizumi Kuonji Temple|2=ja|3=小泉久遠寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koizumi Kuonji Temple|1=Koizumi Kuon-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11462474}}, and {{ill|Shimojō Myōrenji Temple|Shimojō Myōrenji Temple|Shimojō Myōrenji Temple|2=ja|3=下条妙蓮寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimojō Myōrenji Temple|1=Shimojō Myōren-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11361002}} – are collectively known as the {{ill|Fuji Five Mountains|Fuji Five Mountains|Fuji Five Mountains|2=ja|3=富士五山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Five Mountains|1=Fuji Five Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11455915}}.
Although not directly connected with Fuji Faith, Mount Fuji is still widely regarded as a sacred site in modern times. New religious movements such as {{ill|Hakkō Shinboku-kai|Hakkō Shinboku-kai|Hakkō Shinboku-kai|2=ja|3=白光真宏会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakkō Shinboku-kai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五井昌久|1=Masahisa Goi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1534396}}, {{ill|Aum Shinrikyo|en|Aum Shinrikyo|qid=Q217408|lt=Aum Shinrikyo|1=Aum Shinrikyo}}, and {{ill|Ho No Hana|en|Ho No Hana|qid=Q5874143|lt=Hō no Hana Sanbōgyō|1=Ho No Hana}} have established bases there.
== Literary Works Featuring Fuji Faith == ・ "To Die on Fuji" (by {{ill|Jirō Nitta|en|Jirō Nitta|qid=Q3179274|lt=Shintaro Nitta|1=Jirō Nitta}}) ISBN 4167112299
・ {{ill|Sakuya-hime Novel|Sakuya-hime Novel|Sakuya-hime Novel|2=ja|3=小説 咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime Novel|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|Yamaguchi Kaito|Yamaguchi Kaito|2=ja|3=山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) ISBN 4783880204
